plot_summary: Peter Parker sits near Aunt May's bed, contemplating all the problems in his life, but the sudden (and early) arrival of Anna Watson, back from Florida and with Mary Jane, means that May will be well attended to, allowing him to head back to his own apartment. Gwen calls to remind Peter of the farewell dinner the gang is throwing for Flash Thompson, and when he is reminded that everyone is pitching in to cover the expense, Peter wonders where he'll find the money. Perhaps he'll find some pictures to sell Jameson if he heads out as Spider-Man, but this plan seems to go dry a half hour later, and to make matters worse, a chimney suddenly belches out a huge billow of smoke, covering his costume in its grime. Not only is he the poorest hero around, but now the dirtiest as well! When he heads home, he can't even enter due to the sight of his roommate, Harry Osborn, who is shaving off his Fu Manchu mustache, perhaps because Mary Jane didn't like it. Seeing as how he can't get in, he continues on, hoping to run into something that can provide him with cash. Suddenly passing a television building, he decides to try his luck as a TV guest star...perhaps as a panelist on the Tonight show. But his grubby-looking costume gives the executives pause until he demonstrates his strength. Still, they insist on getting his Social Security Number and mailing address so that they can work up some kind of contract, but Spider-Man balks at the whole idea. The executives are soon interrupted by news of a high-voltage cable that is giving the stage crew problems, but one of them, an electrician named Max Dillon, calmly walks over to the circuit breaker and turns off the juice so that the crew can repair the cable, all while somehow surviving the deadly current in the process, which he claims is due to the rubber soles on his shoes. Spider-Man hesitates at the name of Max Dillon, thinking that he's heard it somewhere before, but the TV executive declares he must be thinking of the marshal law officer in the show Gunsmoke and to not worry about it. As Spider-Man leaves the scene, the electrician reflects back on how careful he'll have to be, recalling the events that led to him becoming Electro in the first place. He is met by his parole officer, who applauds his heroics at the studio, noting that he should continue to try and "keep up the good work." When Dillon enters his home and turns on the TV, he finds the face of publisher J. Jonah Jameson, who is outraged about the upcoming appearance of Spider-Man on the "midnight talk show," as he believes he should be in jail. But the fact that Spider-Man is going to be on TV soon gives Dillon an idea. Confident that Jameson would pay through the teeth at the prospect of having Spider-Man unmasked on TV (which he remarks isn't even against the law), Max changes into Electro, intending to actually get paid for getting his revenge on Spider-Man! And with that, he heads out... Meanwhile, Peter Parker laments the fact that the TV studio refused to pay Spider-Man in advance, meaning that he's still completely broke. He stops in at the Stacy residence to see Gwen, and to hear Captain Stacy talking about the scheduled Spider-Man appearance on TV, something he knows angers Jameson to no end! But Peter and Gwen are off to the Coffee Bean to take part in Flash's going-away party, where they find both Harry and Mary Jane in attendance also. Flash partially reverts to insulting Peter in front of the girls, and Peter is so sensitive about his financial situation, he realizes that any stray remark is likely to set him off, so he excuses himself. At the Daily Bugle, Jameson is doing everything possible to interfere with Spider-Man's scheduled television appearance, but soon finds himself in the presence of Electro, who offers to unmask Spider-Man during that appearance. Jameson is willing to pay a full five thousand dollars if he can actually do it, and after Electro departs to get right on it, Jameson's face betrays his glee. Elsewhere, Gwen Stacy chases Peter down, and for the first time, Peter admits the truth of how many problems he's facing. But when he mentions how broke he is, and that Gwen deserves better, she pulls him close and say she doesn't care about how much money he has; he's still the best thing that's ever happened to him. Peter allows himself to be cheered up by this, though he stops short of confessing that he's Spider-Man. The two sit together on a park bench and enjoy each other's company. Two days later, the television station heavily plugs Spider-Man's appearance that very evening, and Jameson surprises Joe Robertson by inviting him to join him in the audience that night - with Captain Stacy as well. But Robbie can see Jameson's delight at the prospect, leading him to suspect something is up, even commenting, "You're too happy. That means trouble...for someone." Meanwhile, Peter Parker calls the studio to confirm the time and location of his appearance, then kicks himself for forgetting to clean his filthy uniform. He can't let himself look like a "slob" on color TV, so there's only one thing to do, which is take his costume to the downstairs laundry room and wash it! But it's filled with people. The same is true at the laundromat down the block, leaving him no choice but to cover his head in a paper bag and swing down in public and wait for his costume to be cleaned as a group of people watch him in amazement. Even though he reflects on the fact that the dryer is tumbling too fast for his uniform to be recognized, he ponders that there's no law against covering one's head in a paper bag, hoping that the gawking crowd will simply assume it's part of a "nutty school initiation." Later, at the TV studio, the callout of "Here's Marvinnnn..." announces the host's arrival, as Jameson, Robertson and Captain Stacy sit in the audience, Jameson in particular watching in eager anticipation. Moments later, Spider-Man swings down onto the stage and begins to take questions, not realizing that Max Dillon is watching off stage. Suddenly, Spider-Man leaps from his seat as his spider-sense warns him of the bolt of electricity from above that just misses him. It's Electro! And in that moment, the name "Max Dillon" finally registers - he is Electro! How could he have forgotten? As the crowd panics and begins racing to the exits, Jameson shouts out for Robbie and Captain Stacy to stay - he doesn't want them to miss a moment of this, which finally clues in Joe Robertson on why he seemed so happy! But far from being overjoyed by the events playing out, Captain Stacy calls him out for being reckless. He knew this was going to happen and risked the lives of all the people in attendance? He has to call it off! But it's too late, as Spider-Man prepares to face off against his old foe. As he dodges one bolt of energy after another, he hears Electro announce that he's planning to unmask him before the entire studio audience. But thankfully, most of the crowd has already fled the scene, leaving only Jameson, Robertson and Stacy. Using his webbing to hurl objects at Electro, the villain is forced to turn his attention elsewhere. This gives Spider-Man the opening he needs to web up his hands and pull them down until they touch his feet. In the next moment, Electro finds himself short-circuited - defeated by his own power! But the electrical feedback knocks Spider-Man off the wall, and falling to the floor, both hero and villain lie motionless for long moments, neither able to move. Finally, Electro pulls himself up, but he's too weakened to make any attempt to continue the fight. He'll have to head home and recharge, biding his time for another day... When Spider-Man finally arises, he sees the entire studio in shambles. He has to get out of there before someone comes along, as he's too weak to take on anyone. But as he limps back home, he despairs of ever being paid. He had one chance at cash, and he blew it! "After what happened, I can never go back! No producer would touch me with a ten-foot pole anymore." Finally arriving at his apartment, Peter looks at his singed and torn costume. Even his hands are bruised. He throws himself down onto his bed and remarks that...maybe he really has been defeated this time. "Maybe Spider-Man's whole career has been just one big defeat...and I've just been too blind to notice."
grade_one_liner: a surprisingly satisfying one-and-done story, filled with comic beats, some decent personal interplay, and naturally, a villain to punch...
subtitle: When Everything That Makes Spider‑Man Work Shows Up at Once
review_text: This issue celebrates the return of John Romita as regular penciler (still with Stan Lee as writer and Jim Mooney as inker), and you can see the difference right away - that splash page was undeniably Romita-like. True, the influence of Mooney was still ever-present, but there was something about the art that seemed to transcend what had come before. (And I want to make it very clear that this is in no way intended to be a slight on the work John Buscema had done, but I remain convinced he was providing only light pencils on this magazine, relying on Mooney to polish things up.) But the issue also featured the long-awaited return of Mary Jane Watson, who by my count, had been missing from these pages for well over a year (since August, 1968 as part of SPECMAG #2, an absence so remarkable that I've been tracking it every month). It had been a big missing piece due to how pivotal her energy comes across to most of the other supporting cast. It was also good to see that she'd grown her hair out again, because at the time of her last appearance so long ago, she had absolutely butchered it. Thankfully, Stan and John were able to restore it without so much as a mention, presumably due to her long absence. (I'm sure some astute reader will comment on the reversion back to her original appearance, but as for me, I'm not complaining! Thank you!) As for Electro, he hadn't been seen since he assembled his "Emissaries of Evil" to battle Daredevil (in 1967's DD Annual 1, where he and the rest of his compatriots were turned over to the police on its page 38). Spider-Man hadn't faced Electro since he participated in 1964's ASM Annual 1, where he was one of the Sinister Six, so when Spidey hears the name Max Dillon on page 6 of this issue, it's no wonder he doesn't immediately remember who that is. It's also worth noting that on page 20 of 1963's ASM #9 (the issue where Electro first appeared), Spider-Man unmasks the defeated villain, but then indicates he has no idea who he is, leaving the mystery of his identity to the police. I got a kick out of the way Electro's alter ego appeared again in these pages, especially after Spider-Man paused inside the television building at the name, Max Dillon, with the executive suggesting that it only sounded familiar because he'd likely heard the name Matt Dillon on the TV show Gunsmoke. That was brilliant on Stan's part, providing both a nod to the popular TV show of the era and reminding the reading audience that Spider-Man had never really met Max Dillon before, other than the one brief unmasking I already mentioned. I can tell you this much at least - I had no idea it had been so long since Electro faced Spider-Man. I was actually quite floored, in fact. As part of the Sinister Six, he had been portrayed early on as a key part of Spidey's rogue's gallery, but that was over five years ago, real-time. Amazing! Stan and John seemed to anticipate this reaction, in fact, and paused on page 7 to review Electro's origin and mention his two previous encounters with Spider-Man. The decision to try and unmask Spider-Man on television, hired to do so by Jameson himself, was played to great effect by Stan and John, and when we see Jameson's delighted face at the bottom of page 11 at the prospect of the imagined headline covering such an event, I actually laughed. It was so much like the early days of the magazine, where Ditko's depictions of Jameson's delight were commonplace, heh. And kudos to artist John Romita too for his own portrayal of Jameson, especially given the fact that the publisher must be very practiced in the art of catching his lit cigar in mid-air, as it falls out of his mouth repeatedly during his brief encounter with Electro, only to be shown in full "chomp" mode at the bottom of page 11. That's some serious dexterity on Jameson's part - Hahahaha! (See what cheap delight you can get from a comic book when you really pay attention?) And speaking of delight, the scene that spanned pages 13 and 14 had me laughing like I had rarely done before, showing Spider-Man at a public laundromat, his head covered in a paper bag. That was hilarious and reminded me so much of the early days of the magazine. I do still wonder what he has done up to this point when he had to clean his costume. He obviously doesn't own his own washer/dryer, so given that he has to have washed it before, where had he done so? But honestly, I didn't dwell on this very long, because I was laughing so hard. The sight of him wearing the paper bag reminded me that there are so many scenes like this still to come (ASM #258 anyone? - one of my very favorites! Oh, and don't forget about ASM #213 also - hahaha!). Sniff...I'm sorry...getting back to analysis, it seems right to question why he thought anyone would be fooled as to who he was on page 14, given that he'd just descended on a strand of webbing on the previous panel! Wasn't that an obvious enough giveaway? It is astonishing how much mileage Stan and John got out of these 20 pages, providing these laugh-out-loud moments, while still giving us meaningful screen time with Gwen, Mary Jane, Harry Osborn, Flash Thompson, Anna Watson, etc. They even allocated space for Jameson, Joe Robertson and Captain Stacy, all while still showing Max Dillon's activities in the background, leaving a full four-plus pages for the actual battle scene at the end! Though the battle itself was quite pedestrian and nothing to write home about, the way the co-creators extracted maximum use of the supporting cast and added humor was a welcome throwback to days long gone by. They even still honored the "no continued stories" mandate, all while leaving an entire page to show the title hero depressed at the end - I'm quite frankly astonished it all happened within a mere 20 pages. Bottom line is that...let's face it...this was the best issue of Spider-Man in some time, and the co-creators didn't seem to waste a single panel. I was flat-out exhausted at the end...having found so much material hiding within these 20 pages. I just can't believe it! I'm awarding this story a well-deserved 9.0, not because the resolution of the fight was clever (because it really wasn't - short-circuiting yourself? Come on!), or for the fact that Spider-Man limped away in the end without ever catching the crook (because that's just par for the course). No, the humor here was transcendent and reminded us all why we love this magazine. And that's in addition to all the personal interplay between the other characters, the nice nod to continuity in remembering that Spider-Man didn't immediately recognize the name Max Dillon, and the return of the old Ditko-like J. Jonah Jameson, always out to humiliate Spider-Man (which he seemed to do every other issue back in the day). It seems as if all the stars aligned this month to provide everything that makes Spider-Man such a compelling book: Humor + Heart + Hardship + Humanity = one very memorable issue! Once again, I remain unconvinced that these one-shot stories will ever work for the company's benefit, but there's no denying the fact that Stan seemed to be fully engaged on this one...and it showed! Well done!
side_note: Last issue established that Peter was staying with Aunt May in Anna Watson's house, so one has to wonder why page 2 shows Anna and Mary Jane ringing the doorbell...rather than just walking right into Anna's own house. Go figure...

If you'll look closely, you'll notice that the book is filled with sentences ending with periods, rather than the exclamation marks normally used in these books. This month's Marvel Bullpen Bulletins mentioned that they were going to do this in a book very soon, and...well...I guess this was the one!
letters_page: I got a kick out of the letter from Stan Reynolds, who demands to know how Spider-Man manages to keep all his fingers straight and splayed out when at least one of them is activating his web shooter. "After many hours of flawless practice, I am pleased to announce it still can't be done." Marvel's response is that, in addition to being able to stick to walls, have super strength and a spider sense, 'twould appear he is a wee bit double-jointed as well! (I appreciated this letter particularly because I remember practicing this very thing when I was a teenager - Hahahaha!) Heh, Christine Cassello raises a good point in her letter when she asks "By the way, is Flash really a name on a birth certificate? I think it is time for you to give him a name, rather than a nickname." (I find this amazingly refreshing, because honestly, it makes perfect sense, and I confess the thought never occurred to me in all my years of reading Spider-Man comics!) She also notes now long it's been since Mary Jane appeared in these pages. Another letter appears from future comics writer J. M. DeMatteis (under the name Marc De Matteis) to indicate that he really liked how Hobie (the Prowler) was given the space to have a real backstory, all to allow the reader to develop some level of empathy for the character. (I might add that last month's Kangaroo was essentially devoid of anything in this regard - bleah.)
ownership:
I was fortunate enough to own two copies of this book over the years.
My VF- copy came to me for a little over $13 as part of the 2000 collection, ultimately selling it for $43 in Dec, 2020, netting me almost $24.50.
The other was a FN/VF copy I'd owned for years, selling for $53 in Aug, 2021, bringing in over $46.
